Steady Course Delivers: Maintaining Conviction with AI Innovation and Infrastructure

By John Hillenbrand, CPA

As we enter the fourth quarter of 2025, we believe our steady-course approach has continued to be validated. Rather than chasing momentum or dramatically shifting positioning, we’ve maintained focus on our core investment thesis: the transformative power of AI innovation, the critical infrastructure buildout supporting it, the resilience of quality companies, and the value of multi-asset diversification.

We believe this consistency, combined with selective adjustments, has been an important contributor to positive year-to-date performance. Calamos Growth and Income Fund (CGIIX) has gained 15.27% year-to-date through September 30, 2025, surpassing the Morningstar Moderately Aggressive Allocation Category return of 11.08%, as well as the S&P 500 Index return of 14.83%.

Economic Foundation Remains Solid

The US economy continues to demonstrate resilience despite evolving dynamics. Consumer spending maintains momentum at 2.5%‒3.0% real growth, primarily driven by higher-income households, which account for over 40% of total consumption and continue to benefit from wealth effects.

Meanwhile, the infrastructure supercycle continues to accelerate across multiple dimensions. Capital spending remains robust, with hyperscaler capex expectations up over 30% since January, reflecting the massive buildout of AI infrastructure. Simultaneously, reshoring initiatives drive the construction of manufacturing facilities, while federal infrastructure programs support transportation, energy grid modernization, and broadband expansion. This multi-year investment cycle creates opportunities across industrials, materials, and engineering firms while supporting sustained economic growth.

The labor market remains fundamentally healthy despite a moderation in job growth. Immigration policy shifts—with net immigration dropping from 3 million annually in 2022‒2024 to near zero—represent a significant structural change that could tighten labor markets and support wage growth, particularly for construction and infrastructure-related jobs.

AI Transformation: Accelerating Value Creation

The artificial intelligence revolution has entered the deployment phase with clear value creation emerging. Businesses with high AI exposure are rapidly adopting these tools—45% of US businesses now have paid AI subscriptions, predominantly for productivity and coding applications.

AI’s impacts span the Calamos Growth and Income Fund portfolio through enhanced productivity in enterprise applications, emerging direct revenue streams, and margin expansion through AI-optimized operations. Critically, this transformation drives unprecedented infrastructure demand—not just for semiconductors and data centers, but also for power generation, cooling systems, and the broader ecosystem of companies supporting this buildout. While depreciation from massive infrastructure investments will partially offset margin gains, we focus on companies effectively monetizing AI as well as those providing essential infrastructure components.

Consumer Divergence Requires Selectivity

The consumer presents a nuanced picture. Top-quintile earners show the strongest wage growth, reversing post-pandemic compression trends. Combined with wealth appreciation, this cohort drives spending momentum.

Lower-income consumers face headwinds from potential tariff impacts, reduced social support, and restarting student loans, although stronger-than-usual balance sheets provide some cushion. The net impact appears manageable but requires monitoring.

Portfolio Positioning: Proven Themes

Our investment approach emphasizes:

  • Technology leadership—companies with clear AI monetization paths, including hyperscalers and software companies, seeing rapid AI-enhanced product adoption
  • Infrastructure beneficiaries—firms positioned to benefit from the multi-year infrastructure supercycle, including datacenter REITs, power generation, industrial equipment manufacturers, and engineering companies supporting both AI and traditional infrastructure buildout
  • Quality growth—businesses exposed to affluent consumers and those benefiting from secular trends, including housing demand
  • Financial strength—banks benefiting from regulatory normalization and yield curve dynamics, particularly those with exposure to infrastructure project financing
  • Risk management—convertible securities providing equity participation with downside protection, while options strategies add income and volatility management

Risk Management in Focus

We acknowledge key risks requiring careful monitoring:

  • Valuation extremes with market pricing increasingly dependent on growth materializing in 2027 and beyond, while momentum readings echo late-1999
  • Execution gaps between AI investment and monetization
  • Labor market uncertainty from AI automation and immigration changes
  • External factors, including trade relationships and credit conditions

Looking Forward with Disciplined Optimism

We believe our steady course has proven its worth through multiple market environments this year. We do not chase momentum; rather, our consistent focus on AI innovation leaders and high-quality companies, through our multi-asset approach, has delivered solid results. The AI revolution remains in its early stages, and companies that successfully navigate this transformation should capture disproportionate value creation.

We maintain our constructive but risk-aware stance. Current valuations increasingly reflect growth expectations extending well into the next decade, demanding continued discipline, but we see no reason to abandon proven themes. Our multi-asset structure, which combines growth equity exposure with convertibles and options overlays, provides flexibility to capture opportunities while managing downside risk. We believe this balanced approach positions Calamos Growth and Income Fund to navigate both near-term volatility and long-term opportunities as the investment landscape continues to evolve.

Global Markets: Diverging Paths, Expanding Opportunities

By Nick Niziolek, CFA, Dennis Cogan, CFA, Paul Ryndak, CFA and Kyle Ruge, CFA

We believe the case for global and international allocations continues to strengthen, supported by exceptional opportunities around the world.

Over recent months, we’ve seen broad gains and moderate volatility, supported by easing monetary policy and fiscal stimulus in many parts of the world. Rising earnings and corporate profits demonstrate that companies are adapting to change and disruption, whether that’s global tariffs, the evolution of the AI ecosystem, or growth opportunities specific to countries and regions.

As we entered the year, many investors worried that “America First” policies and a move away from globalization would exact a steep toll on overseas markets and economies, but the opposite has proven true. Indeed, the policy agenda coming out of Washington has provided catalysts for economies worldwide to move forward with their own pro-growth initiatives, creating many exciting opportunities for investors. Germany’s defense industry is a prime example.

Year-to-date returns across global markets illustrate the breadth of opportunity that has emerged. Many countries and regions are trading at or near multi-year or all-time highs—including Europe, the United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, Japan, and select emerging markets, across capitalizations. We’ve seen the dollar move lower to sideways across most currency pairs, and valuation and interest rate differentials point to further downside, which in turn would support non-US markets.

In 2025, there has been an opportunity cost for home-country bias

Global Asset Class Performance

Year-to-date total return through 9/30/25 (USD, %)

As we look forward, we see many reasons for optimism—and continued upside for many areas of global equities. Earnings growth estimates for the next year are more positive in more places over the next year. Valuations are also attractive in many overseas markets.

As a wider set of opportunities emerges across regions, our team is:

  • Emphasizing companies across a mix of cohorts. Secular growth names continue to provide the foundation of our portfolios. We are also increasing participation in cyclical growth, particularly within financials and industrials and boosting commodities exposure. Our positioning in defensive names is more selective; the opportunities are less attractive.
  • Bullish on Europe and other overseas developed markets. Lower inflation and moderate growth supported by fiscal stimulus provide a favorable environment, including in Germany, the UK, Italy, Spain, the Netherlands and Canada. We’re investing in leaders in technology infrastructure and ecommerce, financials, defense & aerospace, and specialized industrials.
  • Finding many opportunities in emerging markets. Central bank easing, a weaker dollar, and reduced policy uncertainty create a better backdrop for many emerging markets, including China, India, Brazil, Mexico and South Korea. Key themes in our emerging market positions include the AI supply chain, evolving trade relationships (e.g., friendshoring and reshoring beneficiaries), and EM-consumer plays.
  • Encouraged by improvements in Japan. These include a return to higher nominal growth, improved corporate governance, and capital efficiency. We’re finding particularly compelling growth potential in industrials and AI infrastructure, semiconductors and equipment, ecommerce, and media.
  • Continuing to identify opportunities in the United States. Easing monetary policy, a respectable job market, continued consumer spending (albeit with significant bifurcation by income cohort), tax cuts and AI capex support expectations that the US economy can continue to expand, sustaining equity opportunities.
  • Using our proven approach to capitalize on opportunities while managing risks. Our approach combines top-down thematic analysis with rigorous capital structure research and draws on experience earned over multiple market cycles and market regimes.

Calamos Global and International Funds

Our global and international funds share the same experienced team and time-tested process; their different risk/reward profiles and investment universes support diverse asset allocation goals.

Fund Opportunity set Our focus

Calamos Global Opportunities Fund (CGCIX)
  • Developed and emerging markets
  • Equities and convertible securities; all-cap flexibility
  • Equity-like returns with less volatility and favorable skew
  • Companies with higher quality attributes, growth orientation

Calamos Global Equity Fund (CIGEX)
  • Developed and emerging markets countries
  • Equities; all-cap flexibility
  • Participation in quality growth companies from around the world.

Calamos Evolving World Growth Fund (CNWIX)
  • Emerging-market companies and select developed market-domiciled businesses with significant revenue exposures attributable to emerging markets
  • Equities and opportunistic use of convertibles; all-cap flexibility
  • Harnessing growth potential of emerging markets through a risk-aware approach

Calamos International Growth Fund (CIGIX)
  • Developed (ex US) and emerging markets
  • Equities, all-cap flexibility
  • Rapidly growing, higher quality international companies

Calamos International Small Cap Growth Fund (CSGIX)
  • Developed (ex-US) and emerging markets
  • Companies with superior earnings growth potential and demand tailwinds, providing diversification benefits away from US multinationals.

A Precariously Concentrated Top 10 Makes the Case for Quality

By Jim Madden, CFA, Tony Tursich, CFA and Beth Williamson

Good news? Bad news? No news? It didn’t matter since nothing was able to stop stocks’ inexorable march upward. The S&P 500 Index is now more than 30% off its April lows and near all-time highs.

With valuations also near historic highs, what can we expect going forward? Certainly, new highs are possible, but we believe closer inspection is now revealing that the economy and the stock market are showing signs of vulnerability.

First, we have a very narrow stock market, with 10 stocks driving over half the market capitalization gain since 2021. Perhaps even more worrisome is that nine of these 10 stocks are essentially AI-related.

In addition, we have a very narrow economy, with 10% of earners now responsible for 50% of US spending. This is an all-time high, with asset owners benefitting from unprecedented Fed liquidity and suppressed interest rates following the Great Financial Crisis.

So, we have an economy based on the spending of the top 10%, and those top 10% are highly dependent on the stock market. Meanwhile, we have a stock market highly dependent on 10 stocks, and those 10 stocks are highly dependent on possible returns on AI spending.

As long-time quality investors, the Sustainable Equities team has watched quality lag for the past four-plus years. What does the current setup we described above mean for quality stocks going forward? While there are a range of outcomes and it’s impossible to predict the timing of any market disruption, here are two scenarios we consider to be more likely:

  1. All the spending on AI starts to see a return as it broadens out across the economy. This would be positive for quality stocks, given that they have shown the ability to adapt, innovate and deal with the kind of disruption AI will bring. With that history, we feel quality stocks have the best chance to benefit from AI efficiencies.
  2. If the massive spending on AI infrastructure does not produce expected returns, we would expect to see a slowdown in the earnings of the top 10 stocks. This slowdown would ripple across the economy and likely result in a market correction and increased volatility. This type of market scenario favors quality stocks as investors seek the safety of consistent performers.

While our quality emphasis, valuation discipline, and diversification across sectors and positions have not been aligned with market sentiment, we believe the top-heavy nature of the market and economy strengthens the case for our approach.

data.title: McKesson: Strategic Shift To High-Growth Oncology And Prescription Tech Signals Long Opportunity
data.description:
I put a ‘Buy’ label on McKesson Corporation’s (NYSE:MCK) stock after observing its transformation into a higher-growth + higher-margin healthcare-services-platform. My thesis is mostly focused on McKesson's strategic realignment towards its Oncology & Multispecialty and Prescription Technology Solutions segments. The bullish change that emerged from the realignment can be observed in a newly raised long-term adjusted EPS growth target of 13%-16%. The EPS growth is based on a flywheel effect in oncology. Here (in oncology), scale in distribution and GPO services uplift the clinical trial data and expand biopharma deals. So, the margin expansion is coming from high-value access and affordability solutions. McKesson's solid FCF generation supports a capital allocation strategy that directly improves value under aggressive stock repurchases and constant dividend growth.

Regulatory pressure on U.S. drug pricing is the primary risk that I marked. It can compress margins in McKesson's main growth segments. Additional risks (that I consider) include execution risk in integrating large-scale acquisitions [like Core Ventures and PRISM], lingering opioid litigation overhangs, and high customer concentration. MCK stock's strong performance [total return] with the fundamentals attached, I view any possible market-based price pullbacks as accumulation opportunities for a long position. The long position can capitalize on the durable growth that is not yet fully priced into McKesson stock’s legacy valuations.

McKesson’s Upgraded EPS Guidance and Strategic Realignment Signal Solid Long-Term Growth

I observed that the most direct driver for MCK stock performance is the upgraded financial guidance and long-term targets. Following the strong Q1-FY2026, McKesson raised its full-year adjusted EPS guidance to a range of $37.1 to $37.9. This initial guidance was more refined and uplifted to $38.05 to $38.55 during its 2025 Investor Day. This shift in EPS outlook is a clear signal of the forward business's progress. This updated forecast indicates a 15% to 17% Y/Y EPS growth. The growth is 14% to 17% excluding prior-year gains from McKesson Ventures. More fundamentally important is the upward revision of McKesson's long-term adjusted EPS growth target to 13% to 16% and it is up from the previous 12% to 14%. This long-term target signifies a detailed segment-specific growth strategy. To add credibility here, Analyst consensus estimates reflect this optimism, with EPS projected to grow at a double-digit CAGR through FY2030 by attaining a consensus of $64.05. This constant upward revision of both near-term and long-term targets provides the stock with a clear base of forward earnings power. This is a primary determinant of stock valuation. Now, why am I stating this? See the stock reaction on the Investor Day [September 23, 2025].

I want to be direct and specific here. The optimistic outlook is based on McKesson's strategic transformation and portfolio realignment that has sharpened its focus on high-growth + high-margin businesses. For instance, the recent announcement of a new organizational structure [effective in Q2-FY2026] is a main forward catalyst. The creation of distinct segments for "North American Pharmaceutical" and "Oncology & Multispecialty" builds high transparency into McKesson's growth factors. Earlier, the high-growth oncology business was embedded within the U.S. Pharmaceutical segment. But now, as a standalone unit, the market can more clearly model McKesson's performance. The long-term adjusted operating profit growth targets for these new core segments are important to note. These are 13% to 16% for Oncology & Multispecialty and 10% to 13% for Prescription Technology Solutions. Also, this strategic shift is along with the divestiture of lower-margin [strategically misaligned] assets, like the exit from the European market with the sale of its Norway businesses and the separation of the Medical-Surgical segment. So, the portfolio optimization streamlines ops and leads McKesson to concentrate capital and resources on areas with the highest potential for value.

McKesson's Oncology & Multispecialty and RxTS Segments Drive Margin Expansion for FY2026

As I have assessed, the newly formed Oncology & Multispecialty segment can be an advanced driver of bottom-line growth. McKesson has built an integrated platform that creates a flywheel effect. The acquisitions of Core Ventures (Florida Cancer Specialists) and PRISM Vision have expanded this network to ~3,300 providers in oncology and over 180 providers in the burgeoning retina and ophthalmology. This scale creates a cycle that drives distribution volume and GPO purchasing power, that in turn scales patient access to clinical trials. This scale may continue to derive valuable real-world data through its Ontada platform that can then be commercialized with biopharma partners. The addressable market for this segment is big, with the community oncology market alone holding $115 billion. The projected adjusted operating profit growth of 49% to 53% for this segment in FY2026 [improved by recent acquisitions] marks its bottom-line possibilities and its role as a primary base for McKesson's forward EPS growth.

Similarly, the Prescription Technology Solutions (RxTS) segment is also a major source of forward margin expansion and scaling of profitability. The segment addresses complex issues in medication access and affordability that represent a market opportunity of $19 billion. In Q1-FY2026, the segment had strong operating leverage with a 16% revenue jump and a 21% increase in operating profit. The access solutions portion [25% of RxTS revenue] of this business derived 65% of the segment's adjusted operating profit. The bottom-line advantage indicates the access solutions’ high-margin nature. The increasing prevalence of high-cost specialty drugs, like the booming GLP-1 market (which already contributes 11% of RxTS's revenue), forms a durable tailwind for RxTS. McKesson's investments in tech [the acquisitions of RxLightning and FastAuth] to digitize patient enrollment and prior authorizations solidify its market advantage. The updated FY2026 guidance raised RxTS’s adjusted operating profit growth forecast from 9%-13% to 11%-15% that confirms the progress in this high-growth + high-margin business, possible EPS scaling, and the following valuation uplift of MCK stock.

McKesson’s Strategic Sharpness and Capital Allocation to Drive Big Stock Price Growth by FY2030

In brief, McKesson's strategic efforts drive considerable operating leverage. The ratio of OpEx to gross profit improved by ~1,200-basis-points since FY2021. The efficiency is coming from cost controls and strategic investments in automation and tech across its distribution network. Here, new distribution centers are attaining up to 90% automation. This operational advantage directly leads to strong FCF that is predictable. McKesson's FCF may end up between $4.4 billion and $4.8 billion in FY2026. This cash generation scales McKesson’s capital allocation. McKesson has a history of constant return of capital, and now a $2.5 billion stock repurchase in FY2026 is projected [with a remaining authorization of $6.9 billion as of June 2025]. This aggressive buyback program has materially reduced the share count and boosted EPS (and with that improved PE valuations).

Further, McKesson's dividend payments had a recent 15% increase and, with that, nine consecutive years of increases. So, this configuration of strategic growth investment and direct capital returns, backed by a strong bottom line, can continue to lift the stock price higher. Based on 10-Y forward PE and PS cap levels [on these levels, the multiples are hitting resistance] of 20x and 0.225x along with consensus EPS and revenue estimates [from Seeking Alpha], the MCK stock can have a 69.5% and 31.4% positive shift in stock price and market cap by FY2030 [apart from dividend yield].

To verify this with the technical analysis, I assessed MCK stock’s weekly price chart. The price trendlines (grey lines) confirmed this positive momentum with a decisive upward channel. There is a buying pressure at the modified blended quarterly EMAs (green and white lines) that is leading to breakouts of resistance. Projecting the trendlines over the Fibonacci levels provides $909 as an optimistic price projection for MCK stock by 2025’s end [based on the long-term trendlines on higher-highs (originated in 2021-2022 and stabilized on EMAs) and the expectation of a solid Santa Claus rally]. However, using the average momentum [trendlines over higher-highs] $817 price for MCK stock is possible on the upside. If the bullish trend does not progress as expected $715 and $686 are the important support levels in the short-term. As these levels are in line with the pivot of the horizontal price channel and the lower bound of it crossing with trendlines at higher-lows.

Regulatory, Legal, and Customer Risks in McKesson's Growth Strategy

McKesson's major external threat is in the shifting regulatory and political grounds on drug pricing in the U.S. The potential impact of policies like the Big Beautiful Bill, potential cuts to Medicaid, and changes to ASP-based reimbursement models may not be material in the short term. But these issues represent a continued long-term risk. McKesson's high-growth Oncology & Multispecialty segment relies on provider-administered specialty drugs. So, it is mainly sensitive to any reforms that can compress provider margins and incentivize a shift in care away from the community setting [that McKesson services]. Although McKesson is actively engaged in advocacy in Washington, D.C., the outcome of future drug pricing legislation is a big uncertainty that can negatively impact the profitability of its most strategic areas of growth. Any adverse policy change (mainly on pricing) can lead to a re-rating of the stock as the market adjusts McKesson's long-term margin projection.

Additionally, ongoing litigation and contingent liabilities specifically related to the distribution of controlled substances are also a material risk [financial and reputational]. For instance, McKesson had a total litigation liability of $6.377 billion related to opioid claims as of June 2025. A large portion of this is related to established settlement agreements. However, recent SEC 10Q filings state that McKesson "is not able to conclude that a liability is probable or provide a reasonable estimate for the range of ultimate possible loss for opioid-related litigation matters other than those for which an accrual is described." [Page 25] This means there can be potential additional unquantified financial exposure from ongoing or forward claims from non-settling parties. This can result in further charges to the bottom-line and cash outflows. It is obvious, but I am marking this because a persistent headline risk with these legal issues can act as a factor for a sudden price drop in MCK stock [sentiment-based, even in the absence of new financial charges].

Moreover, customer concentration and credit risk are a threat too for McKesson's bottom-line stability. The recent bankruptcy of Rite Aid can be seen as a reminder of this. In Q1-FY2026, McKesson had a $189 million pre-tax provision for bad debts related to Rite Aid's second bankruptcy. [Although management has downplayed its ongoing impact, I do not agree with them.] This event directly reduced GAAP earnings and indicates the financial consequences of a big customer default. As per McKesson's 10-K, its single largest customer, CVS Health, holds ~24% of consolidated revenues in FY2025. I observed that this relationship is long-standing and strategic. But the heavy dependence on a single customer for nearly a quarter of the topline creates a large concentration risk. Any adverse change in this relationship, whether through contract renegotiation at less favorable terms and or a strategic shift by CVS toward self-distribution, can have a material adverse effect on McKesson's topline and profitability.

McKesson's Growth May Face Execution Risks and Potential Price Correction

Back to the main argument, McKesson's growth strategy logically has execution risk. The integration of its major acquisitions [Core Ventures and PRISM Vision] is important to attain the projected 49% to 53% operating profit growth in the Oncology & Multispecialty segment (Investor-Day-Transcript). Business-wise, integrating large complex provider networks can have operational issues, and any failure to realize expected synergies and retain main providers can lead to underperformance relative to current expectations. Along with that, the planned separation of the Medical-Surgical business is a major corporate move. The process clearly involves complex audited carve-out financials, the establishment of transition services agreements, and is subject to market conditions for a good IPO/spin-off. The timeline for the full exit can extend to H2-2027. Any delays, unforeseen large costs, and a poor market reception for the new entity can distract resources and fail to derive the expected value. This can lead to market disappointment and stock price correction as a result.

Finally, technical indicators like recent Relative Strength Index [RSI] divergence and market sentiment suggest that the stock may be vulnerable to a correction. The YTD Total Return Performance of MCK stock has outperformed its benchmarks decisively with a total return of 32.05%. The 14-Day RSI reflects that the stock has spent considerable time in an overbought state in early 2025 and in recent weeks [RSI value frequently above 70 and built up short-term divergence]. This technical signal suggests that the stock's upward momentum may be overextended, and as seen in the technical chart above, the MCK stock entered a resistance zone that makes the market tilted towards profit-taking that can lead to a potential pullback in the near term. I am saying a RSI can move upwards from its current neutral state, and a high RSI [>70] can last in a strong uptrend, but an elevated risk of short-term volatility (should not be ignored) as MCK stock is considerably far from 200-Day EMA [also my model attached above suggests a negative shift in FY2026 theoretical stock price and market cap.

__

Overall, my thesis on McKesson stock is inclined on the Bullish side. The strategic clarity and proven bottom-line growth [~17% Adjusted EPS CAGR (FY21 to FY26F)] and capital allocation [dividends + buybacks] are bases for capital appreciation in the upcoming years. Although the stock may seem to be overextended, any market/sentiment-based pullback can be considered as an opportunity to go long. To keep a check on my MCK stock thesis, I will actively monitor direct performance indicators and strategic milestones under McKesson's guidance and strategic plans.

These are Execution Against Raised Financial Targets [whether McKesson's annual adjusted EPS growth remains within or exceeds the newly raised long-term target, the performance of the newly realigned core segments against their stated long-term adjusted operating profit growth targets, managerial commentary on organic growth versus acquisition contributions in quarterly earnings calls, margin performance, and growth in high-value access solutions and FCF conversion rates]. On the Strategic Portfolio Transformation Milestones, I will monitor progress on the planned separation, like the announcement of the new entity's leadership, initial registration statements (Form S-1 or Form 10), and any updates on the projected completion timeline. I will also scrutinize quarterly reports for updates on the integration of Core Ventures (Florida Cancer Specialists) and PRISM Vision.

Finally, I check whether McKesson is on track to complete its anticipated $2.5 billion in share repurchases for FY2026 (most likely to be done) and its continued annual dividend increases in line with earnings growth. Importantly, every MCK stock bull needs to check on any big legislative proposals related to drug pricing, mainly those targeting Medicare Part B reimbursement (ASP reform), as these can impact the profitability of the community oncology setting [neutralizing my main argument on bottom-line growth]. Also, any new material opioid-related lawsuits and adverse judgments can result in a stock price correction if financial charges exceed currently accrued liabilities.

data.title: SiTime's Growth Story Meets A Profitability Reality Check
data.description:
Thrust of my analysis

SiTime Corporation (NASDAQ:SITM) went public in 2019 as an oscillator-led precision-timing business and has since expanded into a full timing platform. It remains a fabless designer and seller of silicon-based MEMS oscillators, clock ICs, and resonators that replace quartz, with revenue derived primarily from product sales via distributors (and some direct).

Since its IPO, the company appears to have gained market share in the precision timing industry, supported by its differentiated MEMS-based solutions. Yet, its profitability track record tells a different story, with only 2 profitable years post-IPO.

Despite this, over the past six months, SiTime’s share price has been on an upward trend, reflecting renewed investor optimism.

My objective is to evaluate whether the recent share price momentum has run ahead of SiTime’s underlying business fundamentals.

Investment thesis

SiTime is an innovation-led, fabless timing company. It appears to be taking share with differentiated MEMS-based oscillators, clocks, and resonators, supported by sticky design wins and switching costs. Yet the path to durable profitability still hinges on scale. While the company had delivered double-digit topline growth post-IPO, it has challenges translating this into better margins or better fixed cost control. The current market price embeds execution assumptions well beyond the company’s demonstrated track record, leaving no margin of safety under optimistic scenarios.

Growth prospects

SiTime focuses on the precision-timing market, which is a single-digit growth one, as exemplified by the following:

“The timing devices Industry is projected to grow from USD 5.24 billion in 2024…growing at a CAGR of 6.4% from 2024 to 2030.” Markets and Markets

“The global timing devices market size was estimated at USD 5.85 billion in 2023…growing at a CAGR of 7.3% from 2024 to 2030.” Grand View Research

Note that in its Q2 2025 Investors’ Presentation, the company alluded to an $11 billion TAM and $4 billion SAM in 2027.

Notwithstanding this, the company managed to grow its post-IPO revenue at a 19% CAGR (2019 to 2024). This was despite a 49% revenue decline in 2023. Refer to the right part of Chart 1.

The company attributed the growth to more design wins, richer mix/ASPs, and a broader timing portfolio. A temporary supply-shortage tailwind in 2021/1H22 also helped.

The 2023 drop was chiefly a cyclical inventory correction and demand slowdown, after which sales re-accelerated in 2024 on recovering volumes and a stronger mix. Note that in my peer comparison section, I pointed out that all the peers had declining revenue in 2023/24, illustrating an industry issue.

Note to Op Profit Model. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization and R&D.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

I would posit that the bulk of the revenue growth was organic. While the company did not provide any breakdown of its annual revenue into the organic or acquisition components, there was only one major acquisition post-IPO. This was the 2023 acquisition of certain assets and IP from Aura Semiconductor.

That means the strong 2019–2022 revenue ramp pre-dates Aura. As such, it had to be driven by design wins, product launches, and mix within the legacy product portfolio, i.e., organic drivers.

The critical question is whether the company can sustain this relatively high growth rate, as this suggests that it is gaining market share. In this context, the following moats could help:

i) Intangible assets. The company reports a growing patent estate while its platform integrates MEMS resonators with analog ICs and software. These underpin product differentiation that wins designs in harsh/mission-critical systems.

ii) Switching costs and customer embeddedness. Customer design cycles are 6 months to 3 years, with product life cycles up to 10 years. SiTime works with end customers throughout design cycles. As such, its solutions become embedded in customer products, positioning SiTime for next-gen follow-ons.

iii) Fabless manufacturing. SiTime’s fabless infrastructure is positioned to deliver shorter lead times, quality/reliability, and lower cost of ownership. Its platform programmability speeds custom configuration.

These are reinforced by performance leadership in harsh environments (a manifestation of its IP), a distribution advantage (broad channels + self-serve reach), and platform breadth (oscillators + clocks + sync).

Challenging profitability

The company has not had a good profit track record since going public. Post-IPO, the company only delivered operating profits for 2 years (2021 and 2022).

Looking at Chart 1, you can deduce that the key challenge is scale. I estimated that based on the 2024 metrics, the company would need about $360 million in revenue to break even.

Its 2024 revenue was only about 56% of this breakeven revenue. At the 19% post-IPO revenue CAGR, it would take another 4 years to reach this breakeven level, assuming all else is the same.

In theory, the breakeven level could be reduced with higher margins and/or lower fixed costs.

But while the margins (gross profit margin and the corresponding contribution margin) trended up from 2017 to 2021, they have since trended down. At best, I would say that the margins post-2021 have been flat.

According to the various filings, the margin run-up was largely due to volume growth, richer mix, and pricing, plus one-off help from prior inventory accounting in 2019. The post-2021 flatness reflects a normalization of the supply-tightness tailwind and higher industry costs. There were also the 2024 accounting/COGS headwinds from amortization and other manufacturing cost items.

I think a realistic picture is not to expect margin expansion moving forward.

The other characteristic of the company is that fixed cost is a big portion of the total cost (fixed + variable). With such a high operating leverage, any increase in revenue would have a multiplier impact on profits. Correspondingly, any reduction in revenue, as experienced in 2023, would have a big impact on profits.

You can see from Chart 1 that there was a jump in fixed costs post-2021. Can the fixed costs be reduced?

I defined fixed cost = Selling, General & Administration (SGA) + R&D + Depreciation & Amortization. The jump in cost was due to higher SGA and R&D expenses. SiTime attributes the step-up in these costs to a handful of recurring drivers:

i) Higher personnel expense and stock-based compensation as headcount and equity grants increased.

ii) Higher engineering spending for new product development, plus increased depreciation/amortization of lab equipment and licenses in 2023.

iii) SGA rose with commissions, travel, consulting, and advertising

Across its filings, management repeatedly said that R&D and SGA will continue to increase in absolute dollars, with variability only as a percentage of revenue. A realistic picture moving forward is not to expect a reduction in the quantum of fixed costs. At best, expect the fixed cost margin to come down to the 2017 to 2019 average level. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.

The picture that emerged is that scale is critical to profitability. Apart from deriving a bigger profit with higher revenue, scale would provide operating leverage as well as drive fixed cost margin lower. This is good news, as I have shown the company had a good track record in topline growth.

Peer comparison

I compared SiTime’s performance with the following publicly listed peers identified by the company.

i) Microchip Technology Inc. is a mixed-signal/MCU vendor. After acquiring Micrel/Microsemi (Vectron), it participates in MEMS/quartz oscillators and timing. It is a named competitor in the MEMS-based oscillator category and in oscillators more broadly.

ii) Murata Manufacturing Co., Ltd. An electronic components leader (filters, capacitors, resonators). It is cited as a principal rival in MEMS-based resonators, which SiTime also sells.

iii) Nihon Dempa Kogyo Co., Ltd. This is a specialist in quartz frequency control devices (crystals/oscillators). It is a direct competitor in oscillators used across communications, industrial, and consumer electronics.

iv) Renesas Electronics Corp., a broadline Japanese semiconductor company; via its acquisition of Integrated Device Technology (IDT), it offers clock generation/distribution ICs and timing solutions. It directly overlaps in analog mixed-signal clocking/clock ICs that go into similar communications/data center and embedded systems.

iv) Seiko Epson Corporation. This is a major quartz crystal/oscillator supplier. It competes head-to-head with SiTime’s silicon-based oscillators in many of the same end markets.

As shown in Table 1, SiTime is the smallest player in terms of the 2024 revenue and ranked No. 2 in terms of revenue growth rates.

Note that the peers had declining or flattish revenue in 2023/24. This suggests that the SiTime 2023 revenue decline was an industry issue.

Notes to Table 1:

a) To ensure an apple-to-apple comparison, the revenue and EPS were based on USD.

b) Except for SiTime and Renesas, which had Dec as the financial year end, all the other peers had March as their financial year end. For the peer comparison, I equated the financial year with the calendar year.

I compared SiTime's peer performance based on several metrics. I would rate SiTime’s overall peer performance as poor.

Despite a notable improvement in leverage since 2020, SiTime’s peer positioning was dragged down by persistently weak profitability, negative average return on capital, and chronically poor unlevered FCF generation.

It had the largest EPS contraction among the few that shrank. The lone bright spot - balance-sheet improvement – did not offset the sustained underperformance in returns, margins, cash flow, and earnings.

Refer to Charts 2 to 4 for the details.

Notes to Chart 2:

i) SiTime started with the highest (worst) for the debt capital ratio. But this improved so that by 2020 onwards, it ranked the lowest (best).

Notes to Charts 3:

i) SiTime started with the No. 4 Rank for the return on capital, but deteriorated such that, except for 2021 (No. 3 rank), it was among the bottom for most of the review period. It was the only company with an average negative return on capital over the review period.

ii) In terms of the EBIT margin, except for 2021 (No. 4 rank), SiTime occupied the bottom 2 positions over the review period. It was the only company with an average negative EBIT margin over the review period.

Notes to Chart 4:

i) Except for one year (2019 with No. 3 rank), SiTime had the worst unlevered free cash flow margin over the review period.

ii) SiTime was among the 3 companies whose EPS shrank from 2017 to 2024. It had the biggest shrinkage among these 3 companies.

For a company chasing profitability, the peer performance provides the “outside view, ala Daniel Kahneman” on what is possible. In this context, I see the return on capital and EBIT margin as 2 metrics to watch.

Over the review period, the peers (excluding SiTime) had an average median 9% return on capital and an average median 14% EBIT margin.

Note: The average here refers to the average of the annual median values from 2019 to 2024.

SiTime has some way to catch up, as its average on the same basis was a negative 5% return on capital and a negative 13% EBIT margin.

Financial position

I would rate SiTime as moderately sound financially, as its positive points were balanced by the negative ones. Its positive points were:

i) As of Jun 2025, it had $797 million in cash and short-term investments, equivalent to 63% of its total assets.

ii) It had a debt capital ratio of 1% at the end of June 2025, down from its IPO year high of 45%.

iii) Post-IPO, it generated an annual positive cash flow from operations every year. It also had a good cash flow from operations conversion ratio. From 2019 to 2024, it generated $154 million in cash flow from operations compared to its cumulative net loss of $135 million.

Its negative points relate to capital allocation and Reinvestment.

I would rate its capital allocation plan as per Table 2 as acceptable. This is because the cash flow from operations was barely enough to cover its acquisitions and CAPEX.

Of course, this is a feature of a growing company, and in the long run, I would expect the cash flow from operations to increase.

The other concern is the high Reinvestment. From 2019 to 2024, it has had an average Reinvestment of $29 million. This is high based on the following.

I defined Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

Assuming that it can achieve $27 million NOPAT, we have an unsustainable Reinvestment rate defined as Reinvestment/NOPAT.

Note that the NOPAT was based on the peer average median return of 9% with its June 2025 capital of $304 million.

In the long term, I would expect the Reinvestment rate to follow that given by the fundamental growth equation of growth = Return X Reinvestment. Assuming a 4% long-term growth rate and 9% return, the Reinvestment rate should be 44%.

To deliver this Reinvestment rate, the company would have to work on its capital efficiency. At this stage, there are no signs of improving capital efficiency. For example:

i) Gross profitability (gross profits/total assets) declined from 2019 to 2024.

ii) Asset turnover in 2024 of 23% was significantly lower than the 68% for 2019.

Valuation

My analysis points to a company that is on a path to profitability. Its value would depend on the path taken. As such, in valuing SiTime, I adopted the following picture.

i) The base revenue would be the 2024 revenue. This will grow at 35% in Year 1 and be reduced proportionately to the long-term GDP growth rate of 4% in the terminal year (Year 6).

This would result in a CAGR of 19% over the valuation period. This is about the 2019 to 2024 CAGR. In its Q2 2024 Investor’s Presentation, the company stated that it achieved “50% YoY growth for the last 5 fiscal quarters.”

ii) The base contribution margin would be the 2022 to 2024 average and would remain the same over the valuation period. I assumed this to reflect its flat margin trend post-IPO.

iii) The base fixed cost margin would be the 2022 to 2024 average. I assumed that this would improve proportionately to the 2017 to 2022 average in the terminal year.

iv) The base capital turnover would be the 2022 to 2024 average.

v) The base Reinvestment would be derived from the post-IPO Reinvestment margin. The Reinvestment rate would change proportionately so that in the terminal year, it would follow that given by the fundamental growth equation of growth = Return X Reinvestment rate.

vi) The tax rate for the profitable years would be the 21% nominal tax rate.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of $33 per share. This value is before accounting for the value of the tax credit and accumulated losses, which I estimated to be about $4 per share.

Thus, the intrinsic value of SiTime comes to $37 per share at best compared to the market price of $313 per share (8 Oct 2025).

Valuation model

I valued SiTime based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) valuation model as shown in Table 3.

Notes to Table 3:

c. Assumed no change

f. Assumed proportionate change

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

Valuing The Tax Credits And Tax Losses

I estimated the value of SiTime’s tax credits and tax losses (assuming no ownership change) based on the following:

a) The federal losses were about $ 251 million, while the state losses were about $85 million. I assume that there is no expiring date.

b) I assumed a 21% tax rate and a 12% discount.

c) I forecast taxable annual income based on USD 45 million growing at 4% CAGR, considering the terminal value of Table 3.

d) I then computed the annual tax shield and discounted it. Loss tax shield in year t = (tax rate) × (operating loss used that year).

e) I then discounted each year’s shield at 12% and summed the present values to get the PV of tax assets.

Risks and limitations

My valuation is an optimistic one, as illustrated in Chart 5. The projected contribution margin is less volatile and higher than in the past 6 years. You can also see a steeper projected revenue trendline compared to the historical one.

At the same time, the projected free cash flow has a growing trend compared to the one based on the log-normal trendline (shown as a dotted line in the left part of Chart 5).

My valuation model would result in a 13% return on capital and an 8% EBIT margin in the terminal year. If you look at the peer comparison as per Chart 3, these would place SiTime in the upper quartiles of the peers’ performance.

Despite the optimistic scenario, there is no margin of safety. Note that even if I had assumed a lower WACC of 9%, the intrinsic value only increased to $ 40 per share.

My reverse-engineered model of the market price points to very optimistic growth and efficiency improvements, where:

  • Revenue would grow at 51% CAGR over the next 6 years,
  • Together with another 50% lower fixed cost margin in the terminal year.
  • At the same time, there would be a proportionate improvement in the contribution margin so that it is 25% higher in the terminal year.

There is no track record of such a growth rate or efficiency improvement. My view is that the market has run ahead of the fundamentals.

Conclusion

SiTime comes across as an innovative company with strong moats. This is backed up by its broadening product platform and a proven ability to win design slots across mission-critical applications.

Its growth track record since IPO demonstrates that it has been taking share from incumbents, supported by differentiated MEMS technology and customer stickiness.

However, scale remains the decisive factor for future profitability. Despite healthy top-line momentum, the company has yet to establish sustainable margins, with fixed costs weighing heavily and profitability hinging on continued revenue expansion.

Relative to peers, SiTime remains a small but faster-growing player. Yet its profitability, returns on capital, and free cash flow profile significantly lag the industry.

Financially, its strong balance sheet and low leverage provide a cushion, but high reinvestment requirements and weak capital efficiency raise long-term concerns.

From a valuation perspective, even under optimistic assumptions, the intrinsic value of SiTime appears far below its current market price. The stock seems priced for extraordinary growth and efficiency improvements that the company has not historically demonstrated.

Given this picture, I would not consider SiTime an investment opportunity from a long-term value investment perspective.
